text:
In the 19th century women missionaries were accorded greater social acceptance and exercise of function and authority abroad than at home. While the women came to be the driving force of organizations aimed at spreading the Gospel and Western culture in the non-Western world—conducting the vital missions work of evangelism, education and medicine—their methods and purpose attracted much criticism. This paper suggests that 19th century women missionaries, central to what was arguably the most successful era of Christianity expansion in recorded history, are deserving of our research and rediscovery to reflect upon their considerable sacrifice for missionary duty overseas.

Who were these women and men who left the comfort of home and loved ones to serve in distant lands? And what lessons can be learned from their lives that are still relevant for our own day? From the pioneering days of missionary wives such as Ann Hasseltine Judson and her colleagues Mary Guild and Groves, through to the single women of the China Inland Mission and the Zenana societies, women and men set aside the privileges and advantages of the world in order to pursue what they understood to be a call that would last for eternity. As the New Testament puts it, they "pressed on toward the goal for the prize of the upward call of God in Christ Jesus" (Phil 3:14). Through a careful examination of their lives and attitudes, this book reveals that these women and men provide a truly significant model for contemporary readers, both in the developing world and in the more affluent West. Their model of missionary service as a partnership between women and men, together with their unwavering commitment to Jesus and his kingdom, remains highly relevant to the world's largest religious body - the global church.
